content:
4 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Most React Native developers have experienced it.
You tap a button.
The UI freezes for a second.
Scrolling becomes laggy.
Animations start stuttering.
And suddenly someone says:
“The JS Thread is blocked.”
But what exactly does that mean?
Why does blocking JavaScript affect animations?
And what are the UI Thread, Render Thread, Bridge, JSI, and Hermes that everyone keeps talking about?
Let’s break it down.

Understanding React Native’s Architecture
React Native isn’t running everything on a single thread.
Instead, multiple threads work together to keep your application responsive.
The three important ones are:

• JS Thread

• UI Thread (Main Thread)

• Native Module Threads
Understanding how they communicate is the key to understanding React Native performance.

The JS Thread
The JavaScript Thread is where most React Native developers spend their time.
This thread executes:

• React Components

• Hooks

• State Updates

• Business Logic

• API Calls

• Navigation Logic
For example:
const [count, setCount] = useState(0);const handlePress = () => {
setCount(count + 1);
};This code runs on the JS Thread.
Whenever React needs to:

• Re-render a component

• Calculate state changes

• Process API responses
The work happens here.
Think of the JS Thread as the brain of your application.

The UI Thread
The UI Thread is responsible for:

• Drawing views

• Handling gestures

• Processing touch events

• Running native animations

• Updating the screen
This thread is controlled by:

• UIKit on iOS

• Android View System on Android
The UI Thread’s job is simple:
Keep the interface responsive.
Users don’t care whether your code is elegant.
They care whether scrolling feels smooth.

How JS and UI Threads Work Together
Imagine a button press.
<Button title="Increment" onPress={handlePress} />The flow looks like this:
User Tap
↓
UI Thread
↓
JS Thread
↓
State Update
↓
React Reconciliation
↓
UI Update Request
↓
UI Thread
↓
Screen UpdatedThe UI Thread receives the touch event.
The JS Thread processes the logic.
Then the UI Thread applies the visual changes.
This communication happens constantly.

Why 60 FPS Matters
Modern mobile apps target:
60 Frames Per SecondThat means:
16.67ms per frameEvery frame must be rendered within 16.67 milliseconds.
If your app takes longer:
16.67ms ❌
25ms ❌
40ms ❌Frames get dropped.
Users see:

• Laggy scrolling

• Choppy animations

• Frozen interactions

What Happens When the JS Thread Is Blocked?
Consider this example:
const heavyTask = () => {
let sum = 0; for (let i = 0; i < 100000000; i++) {
sum += i;
} return sum;
};Now imagine running this on button press.
<Button
title="Calculate"
onPress={() => {
heavyTask();
}}
/>The JS Thread becomes busy.
While it is busy:

• React can’t render updates

• Navigation can’t process events

• State updates are delayed
The UI starts feeling frozen.
This is called blocking the JS Thread.

Why Animations Sometimes Lag
Suppose you’re running an animation.
Animated.timing(...)If the animation depends on JavaScript execution, every frame must travel through the JS Thread.
When the JS Thread is busy:
Animation Frame
↓
JS Thread Busy ❌
↓
Frame MissedResult:
Janky animations.
This is one reason React Native introduced Native Driver and later Reanimated.

The Old Bridge Problem
Before the New Architecture, React Native used the Bridge.
Communication looked like this:
JS Thread
↓
Bridge
↓
Native SideData was serialized into JSON.
Then sent across the Bridge.
Then deserialized on the native side.
For small operations this was fine.
For frequent communication:

• Scrolling

• Gestures

• Animations
It became expensive.
This created bottlenecks.

Enter JSI
The JavaScript Interface (JSI) changed everything.
Instead of passing JSON through a Bridge:
JS ↔ Nativecan communicate directly.
Benefits:

• Faster execution

• Less serialization overhead

• Better performance

• Lower latency
This is one of the foundations of React Native’s New Architecture.

Why Reanimated Feels So Smooth
React Native Reanimated moves animation work closer to the UI Thread.
Instead of:
Animation
↓
JS Thread
↓
UI Threadit becomes:
Animation
↓
UI ThreadEven if the JS Thread is busy:

• Gestures remain responsive

• Animations remain smooth

• Scrolling continues normally
That’s why Reanimated often feels dramatically better for complex interactions.

Common Causes of JS Thread Blocking
1. Large Loops
for (let i = 0; i < 100000000; i++) {}2. Expensive Calculations
Processing huge datasets on the JS Thread.
3. Unnecessary Re-renders
Components re-rendering hundreds of times.
4. Large JSON Parsing
Massive API responses can freeze the app.
5. Heavy Logging
Surprisingly common during development.

How to Keep React Native Fast
Use Memoization
React.memo()
useMemo()
useCallback()Reduce unnecessary work.
Optimize FlatList
getItemLayout
windowSize
initialNumToRenderAvoid rendering more items than needed.
Use Reanimated
Move animation work off the JS Thread whenever possible.
Avoid Large Synchronous Tasks
Break heavy work into smaller chunks.
Profile Performance
Use:

• React Native Performance Monitor

• Flipper

• Hermes Profiling
Measure first, optimize second.

Final Thoughts
When developers say:
“The JS Thread is blocked.”
They’re usually describing the root cause of poor React Native performance.
Understanding the relationship between:

• JS Thread

• UI Thread

• Bridge

• JSI

• Reanimated
helps explain almost every performance issue you’ll encounter.
The next time an animation stutters or a screen freezes, don’t just look at the UI.
Ask yourself:
Which thread is doing too much work?
The answer is often where the real bug lives.
